codeReview.summary: The feature adds the discover backend commands, DB-backed scan-root persistence, and extensive tests, but two correctness gaps remain in the shipped scan path. `scan_root_for_projects` only inspects immediate children of each configured root, so it misses nested repositories under organizer folders such as the `~/Documents/GitHubMe/minimax-skills` example called out by VAL-DISC-003, and `start_project_scan` only inserts newly found rows without reconciling stale cache entries, so discovered startup results can diverge from the filesystem after rescans.

issues.description: scan_root_for_projects only calls read_dir(root) and then checks each immediate child via entry_path.join(rel_pattern). When a configured root is a parent folder like `~/Documents`, any repository nested under an organizer directory such as `~/Documents/GitHubMe/minimax-skills` is never visited, so the discover scan misses the milestone's own example project layout.
issues.suggestedFix: Traverse subdirectories recursively (or implement the intended recursive fallback) and treat the directory that actually contains a matching `.*/skills` subtree as the discovered project root.

issues.description: start_project_scan only INSERT OR IGNOREs rows for newly found qualified IDs and never removes or refreshes rows for discovered skills that disappeared or changed on disk. Because get_discovered_skills later loads the cached discovered_skills table as-is, deleted or renamed project skills keep reappearing after restart, which breaks cached startup correctness for VAL-DISC-008 and makes rescans drift away from the filesystem.
issues.suggestedFix: Reconcile discovered_skills for the scanned roots on each run: delete rows that were not rediscovered and upsert current metadata for rows whose paths or frontmatter changed.

summary: Fail. The discover backend currently misses nested repositories under default roots and preserves stale discovered-skill cache rows after rescans.
